title: Model Compression & Pruning
summary: Three approaches: regularization-driven sparsity during training, post-hoc pruning loop, knowledge distillation. Path from research prototype to deployable product.
key_insights: L1 regularization pushes weights toward zero — prune near-zero after training
Structured sparsity: zero out entire heads or FFN neurons — hardware-friendly
Post-hoc: measure contribution, remove, fine-tune, iterate
Quantization (separate technique): float32 → int8 for faster inference
These tools bridge research → deployable product at acceptable cost

mathematical_core.structured_pruning: Remove entire rows/columns of $W$ matrices (heads, neurons) based on importance score
mathematical_core.importance_scores: By magnitude: $\|w\|$, by gradient: $\|\partial L/\partial w\|$, by Fisher: $\mathbb{E}[(\partial L/\partial w)^2]$, by removal impact: $\Delta L$

derivations.title: L1 vs L2 regularization — why L1 creates exact sparsity

steps.name: L2 gradient
steps.math: $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(\lambda\theta^2) = 2\lambda\theta$. Proportional to $\theta$ — shrinks large weights more, NEVER reaches zero.

steps.name: L1 gradient
steps.math: $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(\lambda|\theta|) = \lambda \cdot \text{sign}(\theta)$. Constant force $\pm\lambda$ regardless of magnitude → pushes TO zero.

steps.name: Geometric interpretation
steps.math: L1 constraint: diamond $\sum|\theta_i| \leq c$ (corners on axes). L2: sphere $\sum\theta_i^2 \leq c$. Loss contours touch diamond at corners = sparse solutions.

derivations.consequence: L1 produces sparse models naturally. For pruning: train with L1, then remove zero weights.

derivations.title: Quantization — precision reduction

steps.name: Float32 → Int8 mapping
steps.math: $q = \text{round}(w/s + z)$ where $s = (w_{\max} - w_{\min})/255$, $z$ = zero point. Dequantize: $w \approx s(q - z)$.

steps.name: Error bound
steps.math: Max error: $\epsilon = s/2$. For weight range $[-1, 1]$: $\epsilon \approx 0.004$.

steps.name: Speedup
steps.math: Int8 matmul: $2{-}4\times$ faster. Memory: $4\times$ reduction ($4 \to 1$ byte per weight).

derivations.consequence: 8-bit quantization: $\sim 4\times$ memory, $2{-}4\times$ speed, typically $< 1\%$ accuracy loss.

application_relevance: Directly relevant to sellable systems: compression bridges 'works in lab' → 'deployable product'.
notes: Diagnostic monitors from transformer topics (sparsity ratios, attention entropy, head contribution) are pruning signals.
